sections.plain_summary: Mold illness, also called Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (CIRS) or biotoxin illness, is a condition in which exposure to water-damaged buildings — and the mold, bacteria, and other biotoxins they produce — triggers a persistent, dysregulated immune and inflammatory response. In people with a specific genetic pattern affecting immune signaling, the body cannot properly tag and clear these biotoxins. They accumulate, driving widespread inflammation that affects nearly every system in the body. The result is a complex, multi-symptom illness that is frequently missed, dismissed, or misdiagnosed for years.
sections.detailed_summary:
CIRS is a multi-system, multi-symptom illness driven by an abnormal innate immune response to biotoxins produced in water-damaged buildings (WDB). These biotoxins include mold species such as Aspergillus, Penicillium, Stachybotrys, and Chaetomium, as well as actinomycetes bacteria, endotoxins, beta-glucans, and microbial volatile organic compounds (mVOCs). In people without the genetic susceptibility — roughly 75–80% of the population — the immune system identifies and clears these toxins efficiently. In those with certain HLA-DR genotypes (estimated 24–26% of people), the immune system fails to mount a proper antibody response. Biotoxins are not cleared, they recirculate, and the innate immune system remains chronically activated.

This persistent activation dysregulates a cascade of downstream systems: cytokine networks, the complement system, vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP), melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH), antidiuretic hormone (ADH), cortisol regulation via ACTH, coagulation pathways, and TGF-beta signaling. The result is a recognizable cluster of abnormal lab markers alongside symptoms spanning fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, pain, respiratory issues, hormonal dysregulation, and autonomic instability.

CIRS was developed as a clinical framework primarily by Dr. Ritchie Shoemaker, whose diagnostic and treatment protocol remains the most systematic approach available, though it sits outside mainstream medicine and its evidence base is contested by some researchers. Despite this, many patients — particularly those who have been ill for years without explanation — find it offers the first coherent account of their symptom pattern. The diagnosis is supported by a combination of genetic testing, a cluster of objective biomarkers, symptom screening tools, and environmental exposure history. Treatment centers on removing the exposure, sequentially correcting biomarker abnormalities, and addressing downstream complications.

prevalence.summary: Estimated to affect a significant minority of people exposed to water-damaged buildings, particularly those with the genetic susceptibility.
prevalence.estimate: Potentially 2–10% of the general population; up to 24–26% of those with a susceptible HLA-DR genotype who are significantly exposed
prevalence.notes: Prevalence figures are contested and highly uncertain. No large-scale epidemiological studies have been conducted using the CIRS diagnostic framework. Water-damaged buildings are common — the EPA estimates more than half of US buildings have experienced moisture damage — but how many people develop CIRS-level illness is unknown. Some researchers dispute whether CIRS exists as a distinct entity. Diagnosis is rarely made in conventional medicine, so most cases likely go unrecognized.

subgroups.name: HLA-susceptible with active WDB exposure
subgroups.description: People with a susceptible HLA-DR genotype who are currently or recently exposed to a water-damaged building. This is the classic CIRS presentation where ongoing exposure drives ongoing illness.
distinguishing_features: Positive HLA-DR susceptibility genotype (e.g., 4-3-53, 11-3-52B, 7-2-53)
Identifiable current or recent WDB exposure
ERMI or HERTSMI-2 score elevated in their environment
Symptoms improve promptly when exposure is removed

subgroups.name: Post-exposure / no longer in WDB
subgroups.description: People who have left the moldy environment but whose inflammatory cascade did not self-correct. The immune dysregulation has become self-sustaining, particularly due to suppressed MSH and altered cytokine signaling.
distinguishing_features: Persistent symptoms despite leaving the WDB
Ongoing biomarker abnormalities months to years after removal
Often require the full Shoemaker protocol to recover rather than just avoidance
May have repeated failed exposures to 'clean' environments due to immune sensitization

subgroups.name: CIRS with dominant neurological pattern
subgroups.description: A subset where cognitive and neurological symptoms dominate — sometimes called 'brain fog CIRS' — often associated with NeuroQuant MRI findings of gray matter atrophy or volumetric changes in specific brain regions.
distinguishing_features: Severe cognitive impairment disproportionate to other symptoms
NeuroQuant MRI showing atrophy in caudate, putamen, or other structures
Elevated TGF-beta-1 and MMP-9
Often misdiagnosed as early dementia, psychiatric illness, or ADHD

subgroups.name: CIRS post-Lyme (Lyme-CIRS overlap)
subgroups.description: People with a history of Lyme disease who develop a CIRS-like illness, either due to the biotoxins produced by Borrelia itself or because Lyme treatment involved time in a WDB. The biotoxin model applies to Lyme as well as mold.
distinguishing_features: History of diagnosed or suspected Lyme disease
Persistent illness despite antibiotic treatment
HLA-DR susceptibility overlapping with Lyme susceptibility
Similar biomarker patterns to WDB-CIRS

subgroups.name: CIRS with dominant mast cell activation overlay
subgroups.description: People in whom the biotoxin-driven inflammatory state has activated or destabilized mast cells, producing an overlapping MCAS-like picture. This is increasingly recognized as a common complication.
distinguishing_features: Severe reactivity to foods, chemicals, medications, and environmental triggers
Histamine-driven symptoms alongside the CIRS core pattern
May require mast cell stabilization before standard CIRS treatment progresses
Often the most treatment-refractory presentation

symptoms.notes: Often described as exhaustion that does not improve with rest; may fluctuate with exposure levels
Word retrieval difficulties, memory loss, difficulty concentrating, slowed processing speed; often the most debilitating symptom
Difficulty retaining new information; frequently tested with the VCS (Visual Contrast Sensitivity) test
Frequent, often pressure-type or migrainous; may worsen in damp or moldy environments
Diffuse, often migratory; can resemble fibromyalgia
Commonly affects multiple joints; distinguishable from arthritis by lack of inflammatory markers on standard tests
Brief, stabbing pains in random locations; considered a specific symptom cluster in CIRS screening
Often significant; may accompany blurred vision and difficulty adjusting to contrast
The VCS test measures this; impairment correlates with biotoxin burden and is used as a screening and treatment response marker

symptoms.symptom: Chronic nasal congestion and sinus symptoms
symptoms.notes: Persistent runny nose, post-nasal drip, or congestion not explained by allergy testing

symptoms.symptom: Shortness of breath or air hunger
symptoms.notes: May occur at rest; sometimes associated with reduced VO2 max; can be severe
Chronic, often dry; worsens in WDB environments
Related to suppressed ADH/vasopressin; leads to static shocks and difficulty maintaining hydration
A distinctive and commonly overlooked symptom; linked to low ADH levels
Difficulty tolerating heat or cold; sweating abnormalities
Often diagnosed as IBS; may reflect gut dysbiosis secondary to biotoxin effects or MCAS overlay

symptoms.symptom: Appetite changes — increased appetite with inability to lose weight
symptoms.notes: Related to leptin resistance driven by chronically elevated MSH depletion and cytokine imbalance
Often attributed to psychiatric illness; may resolve substantially with CIRS treatment
Difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking unrefreshed despite hours in bed
In extremities or other areas; not explained by nerve entrapment on standard workup
Fine tremor; may be intermittent
Sensitivity to touch; skin crawling; occasional rash
Heightened reactivity to smells and environmental chemicals; overlaps with MCS (multiple chemical sensitivity)
Worsening of all symptoms after physical or cognitive exertion; overlaps with ME/CFS pattern

symptoms.symptom: Hormonal dysregulation — low cortisol, sex hormone irregularities
symptoms.notes: Driven by ACTH suppression; may present as fatigue, low libido, menstrual irregularity, or reduced stress tolerance
Related to ADH dysregulation

key_patterns: Low MSH (alpha-melanocyte stimulating hormone) — often the central deficiency in CIRS, below 35 pg/mL
Elevated MMP-9 (matrix metalloproteinase-9) — marker of systemic inflammation, particularly in the vasculature and CNS
Elevated TGF-beta-1 — linked to fibrotic and neurological complications; often very high (>2380 pg/mL)
Abnormal complement C3a and/or C4a — C4a in particular is frequently markedly elevated in CIRS (>2830 ng/mL)
Low VIP (vasoactive intestinal peptide) — below 23 pg/mL; associated with respiratory symptoms and cytokine dysregulation
Suppressed ACTH and/or cortisol relative to clinical picture — not necessarily outside normal range but abnormally low given the illness burden
ADH/osmolality mismatch — low ADH with high osmolality or vice versa
VEGF (vascular endothelial growth factor) abnormalities — often low, associated with air hunger and poor tissue oxygenation
VCS (Visual Contrast Sensitivity) test failure — a quick, inexpensive screening tool showing neurological impact
Elevated HERTSMI-2 or ERMI score in home or workplace — environmental evidence of WDB contamination
NeuroQuant MRI showing volumetric brain changes in specific regions (forebrain parenchyma, caudate, putamen)

at_home.name: VCS (Visual Contrast Sensitivity) online test
at_home.description: A free or low-cost online test (available at survivingmold.com) that measures the ability to distinguish between subtle shades of grey at different spatial frequencies. People with biotoxin illness frequently fail this test due to neurological effects. Results are not diagnostic alone but correlate well with biotoxin burden and treatment response.
at_home.safety_notes: Entirely safe. Should be done without contact lenses if possible and in a normally lit room. Not a substitute for clinical testing.

at_home.name: Environmental assessment — ERMI or HERTSMI-2 dust test
at_home.description: A dust sample collected from home vacuum or settled dust is sent to a certified lab (e.g., Mycometrics) for qPCR-based mold species analysis. HERTSMI-2 is a condensed version focused on the most pathogenic species. A score above 10–11 on HERTSMI-2 suggests a problematic environment for CIRS-susceptible individuals.
at_home.safety_notes: Safe to perform. Be aware that some people react to handling dust samples. Results require interpretation — a low score does not always rule out WDB if hidden mold is present.

at_home.name: Symptom inventory — Shoemaker symptom cluster scoring
at_home.description: The 37-symptom cluster list from Dr. Shoemaker's work can be self-assessed. People with CIRS typically report 8 or more symptoms across multiple categories. The pattern — not just quantity — is diagnostically meaningful. Available on survivingmold.com.
at_home.safety_notes: Self-assessment only; use as a starting point for conversation with a clinician familiar with CIRS.

clinical.name: HLA-DR genotyping
clinical.description: Blood or saliva test identifying specific HLA-DR haplotypes that impair biotoxin clearance. Identifies genetic susceptibility; does not confirm active illness but is a key pillar of the CIRS framework. Susceptible haplotypes include 4-3-53, 11-3-52B, 7-2-53, 13-6-52B, 17-2-52A, and the 'dreaded' 11-3-52B.
clinical.availability: Ordered through labs such as LabCorp or Quest (HLA-DR typing); most straightforwardly accessed through a CIRS-literate practitioner. Some functional medicine doctors order it routinely.

clinical.name: CIRS biomarker panel (Shoemaker protocol labs)
clinical.description: A panel of approximately 16 biomarkers that together characterize the CIRS pattern. Core markers include: MSH, MMP-9, TGF-beta-1, C3a, C4a, VIP, VEGF, ACTH/cortisol, ADH/osmolality, and leptin. Interpretation requires understanding of expected patterns, not just individual reference ranges.
clinical.availability: Available through LabCorp and Quest. Must be ordered by a licensed clinician. Many conventional doctors are unfamiliar with the panel; CIRS-certified practitioners (see survivingmold.com provider list) are most likely to order and interpret correctly.

clinical.name: NeuroQuant MRI
clinical.description: An FDA-cleared volumetric brain MRI analysis that compares regional brain volumes to age-matched norms. In CIRS, characteristic atrophy patterns are seen in the forebrain parenchyma, caudate, and putamen. Useful for documenting neurological involvement and monitoring treatment response. Requires a specific MRI sequence and software analysis.
clinical.availability: Available in the US at select radiology centers; the scan must be ordered with the NeuroQuant protocol specified. Most accessible through CIRS-experienced practitioners.

clinical.name: Visual Contrast Sensitivity (VCS) clinical test
clinical.description: Standardized neurological screening for biotoxin-related visual processing impairment. Used as a treatment response marker. Automated versions available through survivingmold.com; optometrists can perform it in-office.
clinical.availability: Low cost, widely accessible online; clinical versions through optometrists or CIRS practitioners.

clinical.name: ERMI / HERTSMI-2 (Environmental Relative Moldiness Index)
clinical.description: qPCR-based dust analysis identifying 36 (ERMI) or 5 (HERTSMI-2) mold species in a home or work environment. Used to establish or rule out significant WDB exposure. HERTSMI-2 focuses on the most clinically significant species: Aspergillus penicillioides, Aspergillus versicolor, Chaetomium globosum, Stachybotrys chartarum, and Wallemia sebi.
clinical.availability: Self-collected dust sample sent to Mycometrics or similar certified lab. Costs approximately $100–300. Can be ordered by individuals without a doctor's order.

clinical.name: Urine mycotoxin testing
clinical.description: Tests (e.g., from RealTime Labs, Great Plains Laboratory / Mosaic Diagnostics, or Vibrant Wellness) that measure mycotoxin metabolites in urine. Contested within the CIRS community — Shoemaker does not use mycotoxin urine testing in his protocol — but used by some functional and integrative practitioners as evidence of exposure or body burden. Interpretation is complex and standardization is limited.
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine practitioners and some integrative doctors; some labs allow self-pay ordering. Results should be interpreted cautiously.

clinical.name: Nasal MARCONS culture
clinical.description: A deep nasal swab (reaching the nasopharynx) cultured for Multiple Antibiotic Resistant Coagulase Negative Staphylococci (MARCoNS). These bacteria colonize the sinuses in many CIRS patients, produce hemolysins that further deplete MSH, and perpetuate the inflammatory cycle. Eradicating MARCoNS is Step 4 in the Shoemaker protocol.
clinical.availability: Must be a deep nasopharyngeal swab — not a standard nasal swab. Ordered through MicrobiologyDX or similar specialty labs. Requires clinician order.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: Biotoxin-driven inflammation frequently destabilizes mast cells, producing an overlapping MCAS picture. Many CIRS patients develop mast cell reactivity as a downstream complication, and MCAS can be both a complication of and a perpetuating factor in CIRS.

related_conditions.name: ME/CFS
related_conditions.explanation: CIRS and ME/CFS share profound fatigue, post-exertional malaise, cognitive impairment, and sleep dysfunction. Some researchers believe WDB exposure is a trigger for a subset of ME/CFS cases. The two can coexist, and CIRS may be a root cause driving an ME/CFS phenotype.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Widespread pain, fatigue, and cognitive fog in CIRS are frequently misdiagnosed as fibromyalgia. Some fibromyalgia cases may have an underlying biotoxin or inflammatory driver.

related_conditions.name: Lyme disease / chronic Lyme
related_conditions.explanation: Borrelia burgdorferi produces biotoxins that can trigger CIRS through the same HLA-DR-mediated mechanism as mold. Many people with persistent Lyme symptoms have overlapping CIRS; the Shoemaker protocol was originally developed to address both Lyme and mold biotoxin illness.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: Autonomic dysregulation is common in CIRS, driven by ADH dysfunction, vasoactive peptide disruption, and systemic inflammation. POTS-like symptoms — tachycardia, lightheadedness on standing, exercise intolerance — are frequently reported.

related_conditions.name: IBS
related_conditions.explanation: GI symptoms in CIRS — bloating, altered motility, abdominal pain — are commonly diagnosed as IBS. Biotoxin effects on the gut and secondary dysbiosis may drive GI symptoms that resolve with CIRS treatment.

related_conditions.name: SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: Gut motility dysregulation driven by CIRS may predispose to SIBO. Some CIRS patients have confirmed SIBO that does not resolve until the underlying mold illness is addressed.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation in CIRS can alter the gut microbiome. Mycotoxins can also directly damage gut mucosa and alter bacterial populations.

related_conditions.name: Leaky gut / intestinal permeability
related_conditions.explanation: Mycotoxins, particularly trichothecenes and zearalenone, can directly damage tight junctions in the gut epithelium, increasing intestinal permeability and potentially amplifying the systemic inflammatory load.

related_conditions.name: Long COVID
related_conditions.explanation: Long COVID shares many features with CIRS — dysregulated innate immunity, cytokine abnormalities, cognitive impairment, and autonomic dysfunction. Some researchers propose overlapping mechanisms. A subset of long COVID patients may have underlying genetic susceptibilities similar to CIRS.

related_conditions.name: Chronic sinusitis
related_conditions.explanation: MARCoNS colonization of the sinuses is a key complication in CIRS that perpetuates MSH depletion. Chronic sinusitis symptoms — congestion, post-nasal drip, facial pressure — are common in CIRS and often fail to respond to standard antibiotics.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Neurological effects of biotoxins and inflammatory cytokines on the brain can produce anxiety, panic, and hyperarousal. These are frequently attributed to primary anxiety disorder rather than to the underlying inflammatory process.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Cytokine-driven neuroinflammation and suppressed MSH can produce a depression phenotype. In some CIRS patients, mood symptoms improve substantially with biotoxin illness treatment without antidepressants.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Mast cell activation in CIRS can produce elevated histamine levels, creating an apparent histamine intolerance overlay. Some symptoms attributed to histamine intolerance may actually reflect CIRS-driven mast cell instability.

related_conditions.name: hEDS / Hypermobility Spectrum Disorder
related_conditions.explanation: hEDS and CIRS co-occur more frequently than chance in clinical observation, possibly because connective tissue vulnerability amplifies the effects of systemic inflammation, or because shared mechanisms affect collagen and immune regulation.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic inflammatory states including CIRS can suppress thyroid function or create patterns of low T3 despite normal TSH. Thyroid symptoms in CIRS patients may not resolve until the underlying illness is treated.

related_conditions.name: Mitochondrial dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Several mycotoxins — including ochratoxin and trichothecenes — directly impair mitochondrial function. This may contribute to the profound fatigue, exercise intolerance, and post-exertional malaise seen in CIRS.

related_conditions.name: Cortisol dysregulation
related_conditions.explanation: Suppression of ACTH signaling in CIRS leads to reduced cortisol output, producing fatigue, poor stress tolerance, and immune dysregulation. Often misinterpreted as 'adrenal fatigue' without identifying the upstream biotoxin driver.

related_conditions.name: Heavy metal toxicity
related_conditions.explanation: Mycotoxins can impair detoxification pathways that also handle heavy metals. Some CIRS patients have co-existing metal burden that compounds symptoms; others are misdiagnosed with metal toxicity when the primary driver is biotoxin illness.

related_conditions.name: MTHFR / methylation variants
related_conditions.explanation: MTHFR variants that impair methylation may compound the detoxification burden in CIRS. Addressing methylation support is often part of a broader recovery plan in CIRS patients with this overlap.

related_conditions.name: Fungal overgrowth (SIFO / Candida)
related_conditions.explanation: Immune dysregulation in CIRS can allow gut or systemic fungal overgrowth. Separately, mycotoxin exposure from dietary sources or gut candida may contribute to total biotoxin burden.

related_conditions.name: Allergic rhinitis
related_conditions.explanation: Nasal symptoms in CIRS are often attributed to mold allergy (IgE-mediated) rather than the biotoxin-driven CIRS mechanism. Standard allergy tests can be negative even with significant CIRS, because the mechanism is innate rather than adaptive immune.

related_conditions.name: Migraine
related_conditions.explanation: Headache and migraine are common in CIRS, likely driven by neuroinflammation, cytokine effects on cerebral vasculature, and TGF-beta dysregulation. Migraines in CIRS may worsen with WDB re-exposure.

related_conditions.name: Poor sleep / insomnia
related_conditions.explanation: Biotoxin-driven disruption of MSH and melatonin pathways, combined with neuroinflammation, produces characteristic non-restorative sleep. This compounds fatigue and cognitive symptoms.

strategies.name: Removing the exposure (leaving the WDB)
strategies.description: The single most critical step. No treatment protocol can produce lasting recovery in the presence of ongoing exposure. This means identifying and leaving the water-damaged building — home, workplace, school, or car. For sensitized individuals, partial exposure can perpetuate illness.
strategies.notes: This is often the hardest step practically and emotionally. People with severe sensitivity may need to leave behind contaminated belongings. Decisions about remediation vs. relocation should involve an experienced CIRS clinician and qualified mold inspector.

strategies.name: Cholestyramine (CSM) or Welchol (colesevelam)
strategies.description: Bile acid sequestrants used as biotoxin binders. Cholestyramine is the first-line binder in the Shoemaker protocol. These bind biotoxins in the gut, interrupting enterohepatic recirculation and allowing the body to excrete them. Welchol is used in people who cannot tolerate CSM.
strategies.notes: Requires prescription. Must be taken away from other medications and supplements (at least 1–2 hours) as it binds indiscriminately. Should not be used if still in a moldy environment, as it may increase symptom burden without clearing the source.

strategies.name: Nasal VIP (Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide) spray
strategies.description: A compounded prescription nasal spray used in late-stage CIRS treatment to restore VIP levels. VIP has anti-inflammatory effects, regulates cytokines, supports pulmonary function, and restores MSH. Used only after MARCoNS has been eradicated and earlier protocol steps are complete.
strategies.notes: Available through compounding pharmacies with a prescription. Cannot be used safely if MARCoNS is still present — it may worsen inflammation. Step 11 in the Shoemaker protocol.

strategies.name: MARCoNS eradication (BEG spray or alternatives)
strategies.description: Deep nasal treatment to eradicate Multiple Antibiotic Resistant Coagulase Negative Staphylococci from the sinopharynx. The traditional approach is BEG spray (Bactroban/EDTA/Gentamicin), a compounded prescription nasal spray. Alternative approaches (e.g., xylitol-based sprays, silver-based sprays, EDTA alone) are used by some practitioners.
strategies.notes: Must confirm MARCoNS with a proper deep nasopharyngeal culture first. BEG spray requires compounding pharmacy. Re-colonization is possible with ongoing WDB exposure.

strategies.name: Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide precursor support / low amylose diet
strategies.description: The Shoemaker protocol recommends a low-amylose diet (avoiding high glycemic-index starches and sugars) to reduce leptin resistance and support hormonal recovery. This is used primarily in patients with elevated leptin levels and metabolic dysregulation.
strategies.notes: Evidence base is limited to clinical observation. Some patients find it helpful for energy and weight regulation; others find it an unnecessary restriction.

strategies.name: Antifungal nasal sprays (for sinus fungal colonization)
strategies.description: Some CIRS patients have concurrent fungal colonization in the sinuses. Compounded antifungal nasal sprays (e.g., amphotericin B, itraconazole) may be used in these cases alongside the standard protocol.
strategies.notes: Not part of the standard Shoemaker protocol but used by some integrative practitioners. Culture confirmation of fungal presence is recommended before treatment.

strategies.name: Mast cell stabilization (if MCAS overlay present)
strategies.description: In patients with significant mast cell activation complicating CIRS, low-dose mast cell stabilizers (e.g., cromolyn sodium, ketotifen, H1/H2 antihistamines) may be needed to reduce reactivity before other CIRS treatments are tolerable.
strategies.notes: Requires assessment and prescription. The MCAS layer often needs to be addressed before the person can tolerate standard biotoxin binders or other interventions.

strategies.name: Addressing downstream hormonal deficiencies
strategies.description: Where ACTH/cortisol, ADH, thyroid, or sex hormone deficiencies are confirmed, appropriate hormone support may be needed — particularly for quality of life during recovery. DDAVP (desmopressin) is sometimes used for ADH deficiency.
strategies.notes: Hormonal corrections are ideally done under the guidance of an endocrinologist or CIRS-experienced physician. Some deficiencies resolve as the inflammatory cascade is treated.

strategies.name: Cognitive rehabilitation / brain training
strategies.description: Structured cognitive exercises, pacing, and brain training tools (e.g., online cognitive training programs) may support neurological recovery during and after CIRS treatment. VCS test performance can be used to track neurological improvement.
strategies.notes: Useful as a supportive measure; not a substitute for addressing the underlying biotoxin exposure.

strategies.name: Gentle movement and graded activity
strategies.description: In the presence of post-exertional malaise, exercise must be approached cautiously. Gentle movement — walking, stretching, swimming — that does not trigger symptom flare can maintain function. Graded increases in activity over months, not weeks, are appropriate for most CIRS patients.
strategies.notes: Aggressive exercise is contraindicated in active CIRS and can significantly worsen the condition. Post-exertional malaise should be respected as a signal to back off.

strategies.name: Reducing total inflammatory and toxic burden
strategies.description: Minimizing other sources of toxin exposure (e.g., dietary mycotoxins in certain foods, chemical exposures, cigarette smoke) can reduce the total burden on an already-stressed system. Some practitioners recommend reducing alcohol, processed foods, and certain grains that may carry mycotoxin contamination.
strategies.notes: Diet-based mycotoxin reduction remains controversial and evidence is limited. Pragmatic reduction of obvious toxic exposures is a sensible supportive measure.

strategies.name: Psychological support and trauma-informed care
strategies.description: CIRS carries a significant psychological burden — years of being told symptoms are psychosomatic, loss of function, financial stress from illness, and relationship strain. Psychological support, trauma-informed therapy, and community connection are valuable throughout recovery.
strategies.notes: Addressing psychological burden does not mean symptoms are psychosomatic — it means the whole person needs support during a long, difficult illness.

things_to_avoid: Remaining in or returning to a water-damaged building — this is the single most counterproductive thing; no treatment can overcome ongoing exposure
Starting biotoxin binders (e.g., cholestyramine) while still in a moldy environment — this increases symptoms without addressing the source
Using VIP nasal spray before MARCoNS has been eradicated — this can worsen inflammation significantly
Aggressive exercise during active illness — post-exertional malaise can cause severe setbacks that last days to weeks
Assuming a negative standard mold allergy test rules out CIRS — IgE-mediated mold allergy and CIRS are different mechanisms
Taking medications and supplements at the same time as cholestyramine — it binds and inactivates them
Ignoring belongings from the WDB — clothing, furniture, and porous items can carry mycotoxins into a clean environment
Relying solely on air purifiers or bleach cleaning as remediation — surface cleaning does not address hidden mold behind walls or in HVAC systems
Dismissing symptoms as anxiety or depression without investigating CIRS — treating mood symptoms pharmacologically without addressing the root cause typically produces incomplete results
Attempting to follow the Shoemaker protocol in isolation without lab guidance — some steps (e.g., VIP) can cause harm if done out of sequence or without confirming prior steps

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Recovery from CIRS almost always requires leaving behind possessions and possibly your home. You cannot recover while still in the building, and many items — furniture, clothing, mattresses — carry biotoxins that will re-expose you in a new location.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The financial and emotional cost of leaving a home or discarding possessions is enormous. People hope that remediation, air purifiers, or partial cleaning will be sufficient. They also fear being seen as irrational or extreme.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who fully remove themselves from the exposure — and take appropriate precautions with belongings — consistently report the clearest and fastest symptom improvement. Those who attempt partial remediation typically experience partial or no recovery. The most engaged patients reach a point where they can identify re-exposure quickly by symptom return and exit environments rapidly, which gives them genuine control over their health.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most conventional doctors do not recognize CIRS as a diagnosis and will not investigate it. Waiting for a mainstream physician to identify this condition typically means remaining ill for years.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Trusting one's doctor is deeply ingrained. Seeking outside-mainstream care risks being seen as a 'difficult patient' or someone who has fallen for pseudoscience. People fear being wrong about a complex diagnosis.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who actively self-educate, connect with CIRS-literate practitioners, and pursue the diagnostic workup independently reach answers years faster than those who wait for the mainstream to catch up. The biomarker panel provides objective data that can be evaluated critically regardless of which framework is used to interpret it.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: CIRS recovery is typically measured in months to years, not weeks. The Shoemaker protocol has 12 sequential steps, and skipping steps or rushing through them tends to produce setbacks, not faster recovery.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: After years of illness and the hope of finally having answers, people want to feel better quickly. The protocol's sequential structure can feel arbitrarily slow. There is pressure to add supplements and interventions beyond the protocol.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who follow the protocol sequentially, confirm step completion with labs, and resist adding too many parallel interventions at once tend to have the most consistent forward trajectory. Many report that what felt like slow progress was in fact building the foundation for a durable recovery.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The CIRS framework has a limited mainstream evidence base, and some of its claims remain contested. Engaging with this diagnosis requires accepting significant diagnostic uncertainty while acting on the best available information.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People want certainty, especially after years of being dismissed. Finding an explanatory framework can feel like finding the answer, which creates pressure to treat it as definitively proven. Critical engagement with the limitations feels threatening.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: The most resilient CIRS patients hold the framework seriously but not rigidly. They use objective markers (biomarkers, VCS, environmental testing) to evaluate their own response to treatment and update their understanding based on what actually improves. This makes them effective self-advocates rather than doctrine-followers.

key_levers.lever: Confirming and removing the exposure source
key_levers.why_it_matters: No downstream treatment can produce lasting recovery while the biotoxin source is active. Removing exposure is the prerequisite for everything else. Even partial ongoing exposure perpetuates the inflammatory cascade.
key_levers.how_to_start: Test the environment with HERTSMI-2 or ERMI dust testing. If results are elevated, arrange a professional mold inspection that includes air sampling, ERMI, and visual assessment of hidden moisture areas (walls, HVAC, crawlspace). Make decisions about remediation vs. relocation based on test results and clinical response.
key_levers.timeline: Environmental testing results available within 1–2 weeks. Clinical improvement after removal begins within weeks for some; for others with severe illness, months of treatment are needed before improvement is clear.

key_levers.lever: Completing HLA-DR genotyping and full biomarker panel
key_levers.why_it_matters: Objective biomarkers allow the diagnosis to be confirmed or refuted, guide treatment sequencing, and provide measurable targets. Without this data, treatment becomes guesswork and progress cannot be tracked objectively.
key_levers.how_to_start: Connect with a CIRS-literate practitioner (see survivingmold.com provider list) or a functional medicine doctor familiar with the Shoemaker protocol. Request the full panel including HLA-DR, MSH, MMP-9, TGF-beta-1, C3a, C4a, VIP, VEGF, ACTH/cortisol, ADH/osmolality, and leptin.
key_levers.timeline: Lab results typically within 1–2 weeks of blood draw.

key_levers.why_it_matters: MARCoNS colonization in the sinuses perpetuates MSH depletion, which drives almost all downstream hormonal and immune dysregulation in CIRS. Many patients plateau in recovery until MARCoNS is addressed. It is Step 4 in the protocol because earlier steps create the conditions for eradication to succeed.
key_levers.how_to_start: Get a deep nasopharyngeal culture through MicrobiologyDX or equivalent. If positive, work with a prescribing clinician to obtain BEG spray or an alternative antifungal/antibacterial nasal treatment.
key_levers.timeline: Treatment course typically 30 days; retest after treatment to confirm eradication.

key_levers.lever: Biotoxin binding with cholestyramine or Welchol
key_levers.why_it_matters: Biotoxins are recirculated through enterohepatic cycling — reabsorbed from the gut and returned to systemic circulation. Binding agents interrupt this cycle, allowing excretion. This is often the point at which patients first experience meaningful symptom relief.
key_levers.how_to_start: Obtain a prescription from a CIRS-literate clinician. Standard starting dose is 4g cholestyramine up to 4x/day taken 30 minutes before meals and away from other medications. Start slowly if reactive.
key_levers.timeline: Some patients notice improvement within days to weeks; others require months of consistent use alongside other steps.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Re-exposure is the most common cause of relapse and treatment failure. People with susceptible genotypes are often re-sensitized and react to lower levels of biotoxins after initial illness. Maintaining a clean, dry, well-ventilated living environment protects the recovery made through treatment.
key_levers.how_to_start: Use a HEPA air purifier (e.g., IQAir, Austin Air) in bedroom and main living areas. Control indoor humidity below 50% with a dehumidifier in damp climates. Address any water intrusion, leaks, or condensation immediately. Retest environment annually or after any water event.
key_levers.timeline: Ongoing; environmental vigilance is a permanent feature of life for CIRS-susceptible individuals.

key_levers.lever: Finding and staying with a knowledgeable practitioner
key_levers.why_it_matters: The Shoemaker protocol requires sequential lab-guided treatment across 12 steps over many months. Without a practitioner who understands the protocol, patients cannot access prescription treatments (CSM, BEG, VIP), interpret biomarker patterns correctly, or navigate complications safely.
key_levers.how_to_start: Use the practitioner finder at survivingmold.com. Many CIRS-experienced practitioners work via telehealth, so geographic access is less limiting than it used to be. Functional medicine doctors who have completed CIRS training are the most reliable option.
key_levers.timeline: Establishing care may take weeks. Expect to be a collaborative, informed participant in your care rather than a passive patient.

common_mistakes: Attempting remediation of a severely contaminated building rather than relocating — remediation often fails for sensitized individuals, and the cost and disruption of multiple failed attempts is significant
Starting treatment without confirming the exposure source — treating CIRS biomarkers while still in a moldy environment typically produces no durable improvement
Adding large numbers of supplements alongside the protocol — this adds confusion about what is working, can interfere with binders, and overwhelms an already reactive system
Treating MARCoNS with a standard nasal swab antibiotic rather than a deep nasopharyngeal protocol — surface treatment does not reach the colonization site
Using VCS improvement alone (without biomarker retesting) to declare recovery — VCS normalizes faster than downstream inflammatory markers and can give a false sense of completion
Bringing contaminated belongings into a new clean environment — re-exposure via clothing, furniture, and paper materials is a common and frustrating cause of failed relocation
Stopping binder treatment prematurely — some patients feel better and stop too early before the biotoxin burden is adequately reduced
Pursuing aggressive detox protocols (e.g., saunas, high-dose supplements) in the early stages before the exposure is removed — this can worsen symptoms in already-inflamed and reactive individuals
Assuming that because CIRS is not mainstream, it cannot be real — this dismissal prevents engagement with the most coherent explanatory framework many patients have ever encountered

specialists.role: The most knowledgeable practitioners for full CIRS diagnosis and protocol implementation. Can order the complete biomarker panel, prescribe CSM, BEG spray, DDAVP, and VIP, and guide patients through the sequential treatment steps.
specialists.notes: Directory available at survivingmold.com. Many work via telehealth across the US. Numbers are limited — expect waitlists. These practitioners sit outside mainstream medicine and are not always covered by insurance.

specialists.role: A functional medicine physician with knowledge of CIRS can order most of the relevant labs, coordinate an integrative treatment approach, and work alongside the Shoemaker framework even without formal certification. Many are more accessible than certified CIRS practitioners.
specialists.notes: Quality varies significantly. Look specifically for familiarity with the CIRS biomarker panel, HLA-DR testing, and biotoxin illness — not just general functional medicine.

specialists.role: Environmental medicine specialists assess the impact of environmental exposures (including mold, chemicals, and toxins) on health. Some are familiar with CIRS and can coordinate testing and treatment.
specialists.notes: The American Academy of Environmental Medicine (aaem.com) has a practitioner directory. Not all environmental medicine doctors use the Shoemaker framework.

specialists.role: Not a medical practitioner, but essential for accurate environmental assessment. A qualified inspector can identify hidden moisture damage, collect air and dust samples, and interpret ERMI/HERTSMI-2 results in the context of CIRS.
specialists.notes: Seek inspectors with CIH credentials who understand the difference between cosmetic and hidden mold. Be cautious of inspectors who also sell remediation services.

specialists.role: May be useful for ruling out IgE-mediated mold allergy, evaluating immunological labs, or investigating complement abnormalities. Rarely knowledgeable about CIRS itself, but standard immunological evaluation can help build the differential diagnosis.
specialists.notes: Most allergists are not trained in CIRS and will not recognize C4a elevation or MSH deficiency as meaningful. Useful for ruling out allergy-mediated conditions rather than diagnosing CIRS.

specialists.role: Necessary for ordering and interpreting NeuroQuant MRI when neurological involvement is suspected. Provides objective documentation of brain volumetric changes and a baseline for monitoring recovery.
specialists.notes: The radiologist must be familiar with NeuroQuant software. Most neurologists are not familiar with CIRS-specific MRI patterns — the NeuroQuant analysis software provides standardized output that can be interpreted by a CIRS clinician.

patient_communities.name: r/ToxicMold
patient_communities.notes: Active community of people navigating mold illness and CIRS. Good for practical advice on environmental testing, relocation decisions, finding practitioners, and navigating the diagnostic process. Variable quality of medical information — treat as peer support and experience-sharing, not clinical guidance.

patient_communities.name: Surviving Mold Community Forums
patient_communities.notes: Dr. Shoemaker's official site includes educational resources, a practitioner directory, the VCS test, and patient forums. The primary educational hub for the CIRS framework. Essential starting point for anyone beginning to research this condition.

patient_communities.name: CIRS and Mold Illness Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Several large Facebook groups exist for CIRS and mold illness support. Useful for connecting with others navigating the protocol, sharing practitioner recommendations, and finding peer support during a long recovery. Search 'CIRS support' or 'mold illness support' on Facebook.

patient_communities.name: Biotoxin Journey
patient_communities.notes: A patient-created resource with detailed protocol breakdowns, lab interpretation guides, and community forums. Highly practical for patients actively working through the Shoemaker protocol. One of the best independent patient resources available.

learning_resources.title: Surviving Mold: Triumph Over Illness Caused by Toxic Mold
learning_resources.description: The foundational text on CIRS by the physician who developed the diagnostic and treatment framework. Explains the underlying mechanism, biomarker patterns, and the protocol in accessible language. Essential reading for anyone seriously investigating this diagnosis.

learning_resources.title: Mold Warriors
learning_resources.description: An earlier Shoemaker book with detailed case histories that help readers recognize the CIRS pattern across diverse presentations. Particularly useful for people who are still trying to decide whether CIRS fits their picture.

learning_resources.title: Ritchie Shoemaker MD — Surviving Mold (official website)
learning_resources.description: The authoritative source for the CIRS protocol, including the VCS test, practitioner directory, research papers, and patient education materials. The first stop for anyone beginning to investigate CIRS.

learning_resources.title: Biotoxin Journey website
learning_resources.description: Detailed, patient-written breakdowns of every step of the Shoemaker protocol with lab interpretation guides, treatment logistics, and real-world experience. Invaluable for people actively working through the protocol.

learning_resources.title: Is Your Home Making You Sick? The Definitive Guide to Identifying and Surviving Biotoxin Illness
learning_resources.description: A naturopathic physician's accessible guide to biotoxin illness that broadens the perspective beyond Shoemaker's framework. Useful for understanding the condition from a complementary integrative perspective.

learning_resources.title: Shoemaker Protocol Overview — peer-reviewed summary
learning_resources.description: Several papers by Shoemaker and colleagues are indexed on PubMed, including studies on HLA-DR genetics, biomarker patterns, and treatment outcomes. Search 'Shoemaker CIRS' or 'biotoxin illness' on PubMed for the primary literature. Useful for evaluating the evidence base critically.

learning_resources.title: CIRS Deep Dive — Dave Asprey / Bulletproof interviews with Shoemaker
learning_resources.description: Several podcast interviews with Dr. Shoemaker covering the CIRS framework in accessible conversational format. A good entry point for auditory learners who want to understand the condition before reading.

learning_resources.title: The Mold Summit (various years)
learning_resources.description: Online video summits featuring multiple CIRS and mold illness experts covering diagnosis, treatment, environmental remediation, and patient experience. Free access to recent recordings is often available during and shortly after the events.

learning_resources.title: CIRS Biomarker Interpretation Guide — Surviving Mold
learning_resources.description: Detailed explanation of each biomarker in the CIRS panel, what abnormal values mean, and how they fit the overall pattern. Essential reference for patients reviewing their own lab results.
